turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Our constitution’s bedrock guarantee of equal protection demands that courts carefully scrutinize any law that treats people differently based on sex. Today, the Court contorts logic and precedent to depart from that approach, subjecting a Tennessee Law that on its very face classifies on the basis of both sex and transgender status, and subjects that law to the lowest and most cursory form of constitutional review. By retreating from any meaningful judicial scrutiny where it matters most, the Court abandons transgender children and their families to political whims. The Tennessee Law at issue in this case, known as SB1, prohibits doctors from prescribing hormones and puberty blockers to adolescents if and only if they will help the patient, and I’m quoting from the law, "Identify with or live as a purported identity inconsistent with the minor sex." What clearer reference to sex do you need? What does that mean in practice? Doctors in Tennessee can help a male teenager look more like a typical boy of his age, say, by slowing down puberty if he starts it well before his peers, or by initiating puberty that is delayed beyond its typical start. But if a transgender teenager, identified male at birth, wants to look less boyish and more feminine, Tennessee prohibits using the exact same medicines with the exact same risk for that purpose only. Tennessee is not shy about the reasons for its selective rule. The state explicitly states in its law that it wants to "encourage" minors to appreciate their sex. Is there any other meaning to the word stereotype? Put simply, Tennessee's law is designed to ensure that minors conform to the state’s view of what boys and girls should look like. SB1 ushered in a scary reality for parents in Tennessee. The State’s ban on gender affirming care applies no matter what the child’s parents and doctors think, no matter how dire the need, and with no regard for the minor’s mental health. For transgender adolescents who suffer from anxiety, depression and eating disorders, access to gender affirming care can be a question of life or death. Take Ryan Rowe, one of the transgender plaintiffs in this case. Before he started taking testosterone, Ryan threw up every morning before school and contemplated going mute. For Ryan’s parents, who are consumed with fear of losing their son, and after prolonged treatments of other kind, it was simply not an option to cut Ryan off from his medical care. So along with two other sets of Tennessee parents, they sued to enjoin SB1. The only question before this Court today is whether SB1 must be subject to intermediate scrutiny. The majority already explains that that’s what we generally apply to sex discrimination cases. Applying intermediate scrutiny is the only way courts differentiate permissible sex based laws from discriminatory ones. Under intermediate scrutiny, courts must ask whether a sex-based law is substantially related to achieving an important governmental objective. If not, the law is usually unconstitutional. Because SB1 plainly classifies on the basis of sex, the constitution demands intermediate scrutiny. Recall that SB1 prohibits the prescription of hormones and puberty blockers only if the medicine enables a child to live in a way that is "inconsistent with" her sex. That means physicians in Tennessee can prescribe hormones and puberty blockers with the same risk that it presents for gender dysphoria, but for those children, they can prescribe it to help a male child, but not a female child look more masculine. Just like a law that prohibits individuals from wearing outfits or holding jobs "inconsistent with" sex, SB1 draws an obviously sex based line. As we explained just five years ago in Bostock v. Clayton County, a policy discriminates on the basis of sex when it "penalizes a person identified as male at birth for traits or actions that it tolerates in a person identified as female at birth." That is exactly how SB1 operates. The majority shuts its eyes to the lost text, to logic and to precedent, all to avoid its obligation to engage in careful judicial scrutiny. To get there, the majority abstracts away the sex classification on the law’s face, insisting that it classifies based only on medical purpose and age. SB1, the majority suggests, is just as neutral as a law that allows people to consume Dayquil for coughs, but not for minor aches and pains. What the majority ignores, of course, is that the very medical purpose SB1 prohibits is defined by reference to the patient’s sex. Dayquil anybody can take for the same condition. But here, on its very face, the statute uses sex as the defining characteristic, not the medical use. The majority says that in Tennessee, "No minor may be administered puberty blockers or hormones to treat gender dysphoria," while "minors of any sex" can receive the drug for other purposes. But this sex neutral reformulation does not save SB1 because nearly every discriminatory law is susceptible to a similar rhetorical shift. A prohibition on interracial marriage could be described in the same way the majority describes SB1. It could be read as no person can marry someone outside her race, discriminatory, but not discriminatory while persons of any race can marry within their race. That’s not what the law has said. Yet, as the majority recognizes, such a prohibition plainly classifies on the basis of race and is therefore subject to heightened review. So is this law for the same reason. SB1 discriminates on the basis of transgender status too. The only thing SB1 prohibits is prescribing medicines that that will help a minor identify with a gender identity inconsistent with her sex. And identifying with a gender inconsistent with sex is of course exactly what it means to be transgender. The majority reaches the opposite conclusion only by relying on an infamous footnote from this Court’s 1974 decision in Geduldig. There the Court declared that discrimination on the basis of pregnancy is not discrimination on the basis of sex simply because not all women are pregnant. Today, the majority further extends that illogical assertion, reasoning that discrimination on the basis of gender incongruence is not necessarily transgender discrimination. Unlike women, however, some of whom are not pregnant at all times, all transgender people experience some kind of incongruence between sex and gender identity all the time. So the majority’s logic buckles even with that 1974 footnote as support. Notwithstanding SB1’s classification by sex and transgender status, the majority chooses to subject SB1 to the most cursory form of constitutional review that is only indefensible as a matter of precedent, but also incredibly dangerous. Instead of scrutinizing the legislator’s confiscations with an eye towards ferreting out unconstitutional discrimination, the majority declares it would uphold Tennessee’s categorical ban on gender affirming care as long as there is any rational basis for it. As a result, the Court never asked whether Tennessee’s sex-based classification imposes the sort of invidious discrimination that the equal protection clause protects. It might not, but subject it to the right scrutiny. What is the reason for this departure? The majority says it does not want to second-guess Tennessee’s legislative choice or to resolve disagreements about the safety of medical treatment treatments. The concurrence is to fear that requiring intermediate scrutiny in this case may ask courts to oversee all manner of legislative policy choices. But looking carefully at a legislature’s reasons for acting as our equal protection precedents demand is never needless second guessing. It is our constitutional mandate and it’s our job. Judicial scrutiny has long played an essential role against legislators that want to dictate how people of a particular sex or race should live or look or act. Women, it was once thought, were not suited to attend military schools with men. Men and women, others said, should not marry those of a different race. Those laws too pose what were then politically fraught and contested questions about race, sex and biology. Thereto states insisted courts should apply deferential review and avoid taking a closer look at the scientific evidence. Yet this Court resoundingly rejected that invitation, recognizing judicial review as an imperative check on even those discriminatory laws that were mild in scientific and policy debates. Read the dissent’s description of Virginia’s arguments in Loving v. Virginia and you will be struck by the similarities to Tennessee’s arguments in this case. They’re the state also trotted out a lot of medical information. Here the task of ascertaining SB1’s constitutionality is a familiar one. Tennessee certainly has an important interest in protecting the health and welfare of its minors by prohibiting medical procedures that carry outside risk. In fact, this law may pass intermediate scrutiny. All agree that the state may strictly regulate access to cross sex hormones and puberty blockers to achieve that purpose, as some European countries have done. But none of the countries whose report the chief cites have barred, or the majority cites have barred use of the regulated medicines in all circumstances as Tennessee has. And in the end, none of this evidence changes the Court’s constitutional obligation to determine whether Tennessee’s absolute ban, without exception for even the most extreme need, is actually tailored to protecting minor's health and welfare or instead rest on unlawful stereotypes about how boys and girls should look and act. The Court’s decision to abandon its constitutional role does irreparable damage to the Equal Protection Clause. It also authorizes, without second thought and without careful scrutiny, untold harm to transgender children and the parents and families who love them. In sadness, I with Justice Jackson and joined in part by Justice Kagan, dissent.
